expanded_description:
International trade allows countries to specialize in producing goods and services where they have a comparative advantage, leading to higher productivity, greater output, and increased consumption possibilities for all trading nations. The fundamental economic insight is that trade benefits both parties, even when one country is more efficient at producing everything (absolute advantage). What matters for determining the pattern of trade is comparative advantage: the ability to produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country.

The gains from trade are substantial and well-documented. Trade allows countries to: (1) consume beyond their production possibilities frontier, (2) benefit from economies of scale and learning by doing, (3) access a wider variety of goods and services, (4) benefit from increased competition and lower prices, (5) facilitate the spread of technology and ideas, and (6) raise productivity through specialization. While trade creates winners and losers (some workers and industries may be displaced), the overall gains to the economy are positive, and policies can redistribute the gains to compensate those who lose.

In this section, we will examine the concepts of absolute and comparative advantage, derive the gains from trade using production possibilities frontiers (PPFs), analyze the terms of trade, explore the sources of comparative advantage (factor endowments, technology, economies of scale), discuss the empirical evidence on the gains from trade, and evaluate the distributional effects of trade. You will learn to calculate opportunity costs, determine comparative advantage, identify the range of mutually beneficial terms of trade, and evaluate the overall welfare effects of trade liberalization.

core_concept.principle:
The benefits of international trade arise from specialization based on comparative advantage. Even if one country is absolutely more efficient at producing everything, both countries can gain from trade by specializing in the goods they produce at relatively lower opportunity cost.

Key Concepts:

Absolute Advantage:
• A country has an absolute advantage in producing a good if it can produce more output per unit of input (or use fewer inputs per unit of output) than another country.
• Absolute advantage alone does not determine the pattern of trade.

Comparative Advantage:
• A country has a comparative advantage in producing a good if it has a lower opportunity cost than another country.
• Opportunity cost = what must be given up to produce one more unit of a good.
• Countries should specialize in producing goods where they have a comparative advantage and trade for goods where they have a comparative disadvantage.

Production Possibilities Frontier (PPF):
• Shows the maximum combinations of two goods a country can produce given its resources and technology.
• The slope of the PPF is the opportunity cost.
• Without trade, consumption is constrained by the PPF.
• With trade, consumption can be outside the PPF (gains from trade).

Terms of Trade:
• The ratio at which a country exports one good in exchange for another.
• Mutually beneficial terms of trade lie between the two countries' opportunity costs.
• Example: If Country A's opportunity cost of wheat is 2 cloth, and Country B's opportunity cost of wheat is 4 cloth, any terms of trade between 2 and 4 cloth per wheat benefits both.

Gains from Trade:
1. Consumption gains: Countries can consume outside their PPF.
2. Production gains: Specialization increases total output.
3. Efficiency gains: Resources are allocated to their most productive uses.
4. Dynamic gains: Economies of scale, learning by doing, technology transfer.

Ricardian Model:
• Comparative advantage driven by differences in technology (labor productivity).
• Predicts complete specialization.
• Gains from trade are unambiguous.

Heckscher-Ohlin Model:
• Comparative advantage driven by differences in factor endowments (capital, labor, land).
• Countries export goods that use their abundant factors intensively.
• Predicts incomplete specialization.

Distributional Effects:
• Trade creates winners and losers within countries.
• Owners of abundant factors gain; owners of scarce factors lose (Stolper-Samuelson theorem).
• Workers in import-competing industries may lose jobs.
• Policies (retraining, unemployment insurance, trade adjustment assistance) can redistribute gains.

Empirical Evidence:
• Countries that trade more grow faster (though causality is debated).
• Trade liberalization raises overall welfare.
• The gains from trade are substantial but not infinite; estimates range from 1-10% of GDP.
• The costs of trade (job displacement) are concentrated but temporary.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine two countries: Portugal and England. Both produce wine and cloth. Portugal can produce both wine and cloth more efficiently than England (absolute advantage). But Portugal is relatively better at producing wine (it has a comparative advantage in wine), and England is relatively better at producing cloth (it has a comparative advantage in cloth).

If both countries specialize—Portugal producing only wine, England producing only cloth—and trade, total output of both goods increases. Portugal can export wine to England and import cloth. Both countries can consume more of both goods than if they produced both themselves.

The gains from trade come from specialization. When each country does what it does relatively best, total productivity increases. The same resources produce more output. This is the principle of comparative advantage, first articulated by David Ricardo in 1817.

Trade also brings dynamic gains: larger markets allow firms to achieve economies of scale, reducing costs. Competition from foreign firms forces domestic firms to become more efficient. Technology and ideas flow across borders. Consumers get access to a wider variety of goods at lower prices.

However, trade also creates losers. Workers in import-competing industries may lose jobs. In the US, manufacturing employment has declined as trade with low-wage countries increased. But these losses are not a reason to stop trade; they are a reason to have policies that help displaced workers (retraining, unemployment benefits, relocation assistance). The overall gains from trade are large enough to compensate the losers, though compensation is not automatic.

image.alt: PPF diagram for two countries showing specialization and gains from trade; consumption points outside PPF after trade.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.1: Gains from trade: through specialization and exchange, both countries can consume outside their production possibilities frontiers.

sections.introduction:
International trade allows countries to specialize in producing goods and services where they have a comparative advantage, leading to higher productivity, greater output, and increased consumption possibilities for all trading nations. The fundamental economic insight is that trade benefits both parties, even when one country is more efficient at producing everything (absolute advantage). What matters for determining the pattern of trade is comparative advantage: the ability to produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country.

The gains from trade are substantial and well-documented. Trade allows countries to: (1) consume beyond their production possibilities frontier, (2) benefit from economies of scale and learning by doing, (3) access a wider variety of goods and services, (4) benefit from increased competition and lower prices, (5) facilitate the spread of technology and ideas, and (6) raise productivity through specialization. While trade creates winners and losers (some workers and industries may be displaced), the overall gains to the economy are positive, and policies can redistribute the gains to compensate those who lose.

In this section, we will examine the concepts of absolute and comparative advantage, derive the gains from trade using production possibilities frontiers (PPFs), analyze the terms of trade, explore the sources of comparative advantage, discuss the empirical evidence on the gains from trade, and evaluate the distributional effects of trade.

introduction_image.alt: World map showing trade flows; graph of world GDP over time showing increase with trade liberalization.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 11.1.2: Global trade has grown dramatically over time, contributing to rising living standards worldwide.

key_concept.definition:
International Trade: The exchange of goods, services, and capital across international borders.

Absolute Advantage: A country has an absolute advantage in producing a good if it can produce more output per unit of input (or use fewer inputs per unit of output) than another country.

Comparative Advantage: A country has a comparative advantage in producing a good if it has a lower opportunity cost than another country. This is the fundamental basis for mutually beneficial trade.

Opportunity Cost: The value of the next best alternative foregone when producing a good. For example, the opportunity cost of producing 1 unit of wheat is the amount of cloth that could have been produced with the same resources.

Production Possibilities Frontier (PPF): A curve showing the maximum combinations of two goods that a country can produce given its resources and technology. The slope of the PPF is the opportunity cost.

Terms of Trade: The ratio at which a country exports one good in exchange for another. For mutually beneficial trade, the terms of trade must lie between the two countries' opportunity costs.

Gains from Trade:
1. Consumption gains: Countries can consume outside their PPF.
2. Production gains: Specialization increases total output.
3. Efficiency gains: Resources allocated to highest-value uses.
4. Dynamic gains: Economies of scale, learning, technology transfer.

Ricardian Model: Comparative advantage driven by differences in labor productivity (technology). Predicts complete specialization.

Heckscher-Ohlin Model: Comparative advantage driven by differences in factor endowments (capital, labor, land). Countries export goods that use their abundant factors intensively.

Stolper-Samuelson Theorem: Trade benefits owners of a country's abundant factors and harms owners of scarce factors. For example, in a capital-abundant country, capitalists gain from trade; workers lose.

Distributional Effects: Trade creates winners and losers within countries. Policies (trade adjustment assistance, retraining, unemployment insurance) can redistribute gains to compensate losers.
key_concept.context: The theory of comparative advantage was developed by David Ricardo (1817) in his book Principles of Political Economy and Taxation. The famous example of Portugal and England trading wine and cloth demonstrated that trade benefits both countries even if Portugal is more efficient at producing both goods. The Heckscher-Ohlin model (1919, 1933) added factor endowments as a source of comparative advantage. Empirical evidence generally supports the gains from trade, though the magnitude of gains is debated. The post-WWII period saw dramatic trade liberalization (GATT, WTO, regional trade agreements), coinciding with rising global living standards. However, trade also creates adjustment costs; the debate over trade policy often centers on how to manage these costs.

image.alt: David Ricardo portrait; PPF diagram for Portugal and England showing comparative advantage in wine and cloth.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.3: David Ricardo's theory of comparative advantage (1817) showed that trade benefits all countries, even if one is absolutely more efficient at everything.

classifications.value: Absolute advantage measures productivity; comparative advantage measures opportunity cost and determines trade patterns
characteristics: Absolute advantage: higher output per input or lower input per output.
A country can have absolute advantage in all goods but still gain from trade.
Comparative advantage is the basis for mutually beneficial trade.
Example: Portugal has absolute advantage in both wine and cloth, but comparative advantage in wine (lower opportunity cost).
Countries should specialize where they have comparative advantage.
classifications.behavior:
The distinction between absolute and comparative advantage is crucial. Many people mistakenly believe that trade only benefits countries that are 'better' at producing something. But Ricardo showed that even a country that is worse at producing everything can still gain from trade by specializing in the good where it is least bad (has comparative advantage).

Example: Portugal can produce 10 units of wine or 10 units of cloth with one unit of labor. England can produce 2 units of wine or 4 units of cloth. Portugal has absolute advantage in both (10 > 2, 10 > 4). But Portugal's opportunity cost of wine is 1 cloth (10/10). England's opportunity cost of wine is 2 cloth (4/2 = 2). Portugal has lower opportunity cost in wine (comparative advantage). England has lower opportunity cost in cloth (1/2 wine vs. 1 wine? Actually England's opportunity cost of cloth is 0.5 wine, Portugal's is 1 wine). So Portugal should specialize in wine, England in cloth. Both gain from trade.

image.alt: Table: Portugal (wine:10, cloth:10), England (wine:2, cloth:4); opportunity cost calculations; comparative advantage determination.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.4: Absolute advantage (Portugal) vs. comparative advantage (Portugal in wine, England in cloth). Trade benefits both.

examples.explanation: Portugal: 1 unit of labor produces 10 wine OR 10 cloth. England: 1 unit of labor produces 2 wine OR 4 cloth. Portugal has absolute advantage in both. Portugal's opportunity cost of wine = 1 cloth. England's opportunity cost of wine = 2 cloth. Portugal has comparative advantage in wine. England's opportunity cost of cloth = 0.5 wine. Portugal's opportunity cost of cloth = 1 wine. England has comparative advantage in cloth. Trade benefits both.

The US has absolute advantage in many high-tech goods (aircraft, software). China has comparative advantage in labor-intensive goods (textiles, electronics assembly). Both gain from trade: US exports aircraft, imports textiles; China exports textiles, imports aircraft. Total output increases.

classifications.type: Terms of Trade and Gains from Trade
classifications.value: The terms of trade determine how the gains from trade are divided between trading partners
characteristics: Terms of trade = price of exports / price of imports.
Mutually beneficial terms of trade lie between opportunity costs.
If terms of trade are closer to Country A's opportunity cost, Country B gains more (and vice versa).
Example: Portugal's opportunity cost of wine = 1 cloth; England's = 2 cloth. Any terms of trade between 1 and 2 cloth per wine benefits both.

If terms of trade = 1.5 cloth per wine, Portugal gets 1.5 cloth for each wine (better than 1), England gets 1.5 cloth per wine (better than 2 from producing itself?). Actually England gives up 1.5 cloth for 1 wine, which is better than producing wine itself (which costs 2 cloth). So both gain.
The range of possible gains is determined by opportunity costs.
classifications.behavior:
The terms of trade determine how the gains from trade are divided. If the terms of trade are closer to one country's opportunity cost, that country gains less (because it is trading at a price close to its own cost), and the other country gains more.

In the Portugal-England example, Portugal's opportunity cost of wine is 1 cloth; England's is 2 cloth. If the terms of trade are 1.1 cloth per wine, Portugal gains a little (gets 1.1 instead of 1), England gains a lot (gets wine for 1.1 cloth instead of producing it for 2 cloth). If the terms of trade are 1.9 cloth per wine, Portugal gains a lot, England gains a little.

The actual terms of trade are determined by supply and demand in international markets. Countries with market power (large economies) can influence the terms of trade. A large country that imposes a tariff can improve its terms of trade (at the expense of its trading partners), but this causes deadweight loss and may lead to retaliation.

The gains from trade are not just about consumption; they also include dynamic gains from economies of scale, learning, and technology transfer. These dynamic gains are often larger than the static gains measured by PPF shifts.

image.alt: Graph: Range of mutually beneficial terms of trade between opportunity costs; division of gains shown.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.5: Mutually beneficial terms of trade lie between the two countries' opportunity costs. The actual terms of trade determine how the gains are divided.

examples.explanation: Portugal: opp cost wine = 1 cloth. England: opp cost wine = 2 cloth. Mutually beneficial terms of trade: 1 < TOT < 2 (cloth per wine). If TOT = 1.5, Portugal exports 10 wine for 15 cloth. Before trade, Portugal could produce 10 wine and 0 cloth, or 0 wine and 10 cloth. After trade, Portugal can have 10 wine and 15 cloth (by producing 10 wine, exporting all, importing 15 cloth). This is outside its PPF (which maxes at 10 cloth if producing 10 wine). England: produces 40 cloth (using 10 labor), exports 15 cloth, imports 10 wine. Before trade, England could have at most 20 cloth and 10 wine (if it produced both). After trade, it has 25 cloth and 10 wine, also outside its PPF. Both gain.

If TOT = 1.1, Portugal gets 11 cloth for 10 wine (gain of 1 cloth), England gets wine for 1.1 cloth (gain of 0.9 cloth per wine). Portugal gains less; England gains more. If TOT = 1.9, Portugal gains more, England gains less. The terms of trade matter for distribution.

classifications.value: Comparative advantage arises from differences in technology (Ricardo) or factor endowments (Heckscher-Ohlin)
characteristics: Ricardian model: differences in labor productivity (technology).
Heckscher-Ohlin model: differences in factor endowments (capital, labor, land).
Countries export goods that use their abundant factors intensively.
Example: Capital-abundant countries export capital-intensive goods; labor-abundant countries export labor-intensive goods.
The Stolper-Samuelson theorem: trade benefits owners of abundant factors, harms owners of scarce factors.
Empirical evidence supports both models, though Heckscher-Ohlin is more relevant for explaining trade patterns between developed and developing countries.
classifications.behavior:
Why do countries have different opportunity costs? There are two main explanations. The Ricardian model emphasizes differences in technology. Some countries are better at producing certain goods because they have more advanced technology, better infrastructure, or more skilled workers. The Heckscher-Ohlin model emphasizes differences in factor endowments. Countries with abundant capital (developed countries) export capital-intensive goods (machinery, chemicals). Countries with abundant labor (developing countries) export labor-intensive goods (textiles, assembled electronics). Countries with abundant land export agricultural products.

Both models are supported by evidence. The US exports capital-intensive goods (aircraft, semiconductors) and imports labor-intensive goods (apparel, toys). China exports labor-intensive goods and imports capital-intensive goods. As China has accumulated capital, its export mix has shifted toward more capital-intensive goods (the Leontief paradox? Actually the Leontief paradox found that the US exported labor-intensive goods in the 1950s, which seemed to contradict Heckscher-Ohlin. This was resolved by recognizing that US labor is highly skilled; when accounting for skill, the US exports skill-intensive goods.)

New trade theory adds economies of scale and product differentiation as sources of trade (intra-industry trade). Countries with similar factor endowments trade differentiated products (e.g., Germany exports cars to France and imports cars from France). This type of trade is driven by consumer preferences for variety and economies of scale, not comparative advantage.

image.alt: Diagram: Ricardian (technology differences) and Heckscher-Ohlin (factor endowment differences) as sources of comparative advantage.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.6: Comparative advantage arises from differences in technology (Ricardo) or factor endowments (Heckscher-Ohlin).

examples.explanation: The US is capital-abundant; China is labor-abundant. The US exports capital-intensive goods (aircraft, software, machinery). China exports labor-intensive goods (textiles, electronics assembly, toys). This matches Heckscher-Ohlin predictions.

Germany exports cars to France and imports cars from France. Both countries have similar factor endowments. This trade is driven by product differentiation (consumers want variety) and economies of scale. It is not explained by comparative advantage but by new trade theory.

principles.principle: Trade Benefits All Countries, But the Gains Are Not Automatically Distributed Equally Within Countries
principles.statement: The principle of comparative advantage shows that all countries can gain from trade in aggregate. However, within each country, trade creates winners and losers. Owners of factors that are abundant in the country gain; owners of scarce factors lose (Stolper-Samuelson theorem). Workers in export industries gain; workers in import-competing industries may lose jobs. The overall gain is positive, but the distributional effects can be significant. This is why trade is often politically controversial. The solution is not to restrict trade (which would reduce overall welfare) but to use domestic policies (retraining, unemployment insurance, trade adjustment assistance, education) to help those who lose from trade. The gains from trade are large enough to compensate the losers, though compensation is not automatic. In practice, many countries have programs to assist displaced workers, but these programs are often underfunded.
principles.application: Policymakers should recognize that trade creates both gains and adjustment costs. They should pursue trade liberalization to capture the gains, but also implement policies to help displaced workers. These policies include: trade adjustment assistance (cash benefits, retraining), unemployment insurance, relocation assistance, education and training programs, and wage insurance (to compensate workers who take lower-paying jobs). The gains from trade are large enough to fund these programs. Without such programs, political opposition to trade may prevent liberalization, leaving everyone worse off.
principles.example: The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) increased trade between the US, Canada, and Mexico. Overall, all three countries gained. However, some US manufacturing workers lost jobs as production moved to Mexico. The US had Trade Adjustment Assistance (TAA) programs to help these workers, but the programs were underfunded and many workers struggled. The political backlash against NAFTA was strong, contributing to anti-trade sentiment. This illustrates the importance of pairing trade liberalization with robust adjustment assistance.

image.alt: Graph: Winners and losers from trade within a country; overall gain positive but distribution uneven.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.7: Trade creates winners and losers within countries. The overall gain is positive, but the distributional effects can be significant.

principles.principle: The Gains from Trade Extend Beyond Static Efficiency to Dynamic Benefits (Economies of Scale, Learning, Technology Transfer)
principles.statement: The static gains from trade (consumption outside the PPF) are only part of the story. Trade also brings dynamic gains: larger markets allow firms to achieve economies of scale, reducing average costs. Increased competition forces domestic firms to become more efficient. Trade facilitates the flow of technology and ideas across borders (technology transfer). Learning by doing (as firms produce more, they become more efficient) is also enhanced by access to larger markets. These dynamic gains are often larger than the static gains and are more difficult to measure. Empirical studies suggest that the dynamic gains from trade can be 5-10 times larger than the static gains. This is why trade is so important for economic development: countries that open up to trade tend to grow faster, though causality is debated.
principles.application: Policymakers should consider not just the static gains from trade but also the dynamic benefits. Trade liberalization can boost long-run growth by increasing competition, facilitating technology transfer, and allowing firms to achieve scale economies. This is particularly important for developing countries, which can learn from more advanced economies. However, the dynamic gains take time to materialize and require complementary policies (education, infrastructure, rule of law) to be realized.
principles.example: China's growth since joining the WTO in 2001 is often cited as an example of dynamic gains from trade. Access to global markets allowed Chinese firms to achieve economies of scale, attract foreign direct investment (which brought technology), and learn from more advanced producers. China's GDP grew at 10% per year for two decades, lifting hundreds of millions out of poverty. While other factors contributed, trade was a major driver.

image.alt: Graph: Average cost falling as output increases (economies of scale); learning curve showing cost reduction over time.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.8: Dynamic gains from trade include economies of scale and learning by doing, which reduce costs and increase productivity over time.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Comparative Advantage and Gains from Trade

image.alt: Table: US (wheat:100, cloth:200), UK (wheat:50, cloth:100). Opportunity cost calculations; comparative advantage; terms of trade.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.9: Worked example: calculating opportunity costs, determining comparative advantage, and finding mutually beneficial terms of trade.

worked_examples.problem:
Two countries, the US and the UK, produce two goods: wheat and cloth. The following table shows the output per unit of labor:

| Country | Wheat | Cloth |
|---------|-------|-------|
| US | 100 | 200 |
| UK | 50 | 100 |

Assume each country has 10 units of labor.

a) Which country has an absolute advantage in wheat? In cloth?
b) Calculate the opportunity cost of wheat in each country. Which country has a comparative advantage in wheat?
c) Calculate the opportunity cost of cloth in each country. Which country has a comparative advantage in cloth?
d) If both countries specialize completely according to comparative advantage, what will be total world output of each good?
e) If the terms of trade are 1.5 cloth per wheat, show that both countries gain from trade.
f) What is the range of mutually beneficial terms of trade?

step_by_step.explanation:
US: 100 wheat > 50 wheat, so US has absolute advantage in wheat.
US: 200 cloth > 100 cloth, so US has absolute advantage in cloth.
US has absolute advantage in both goods.

US: To produce 1 wheat, the US gives up 200/100 = 2 cloth.
UK: To produce 1 wheat, the UK gives up 100/50 = 2 cloth.
Both have the same opportunity cost (2 cloth per wheat).

This means there is no comparative advantage; opportunity costs are equal. Trade would not be beneficial (or would be arbitrary).

The problem as written has equal opportunity costs (2 cloth per wheat for both). This is a special case. Let's adjust the UK cloth output to 80 (instead of 100) to create a difference.

New data:
US: 100 wheat, 200 cloth → opp cost wheat = 2 cloth, opp cost cloth = 0.5 wheat.
UK: 50 wheat, 80 cloth → opp cost wheat = 80/50 = 1.6 cloth, opp cost cloth = 50/80 = 0.625 wheat.

Now UK has lower opportunity cost in wheat (1.6 < 2) → comparative advantage in wheat.
US has lower opportunity cost in cloth (0.5 < 0.625) → comparative advantage in cloth.

This is the correct setup for comparative advantage.

step_by_step.step: Total world output with specialization (using corrected data)
step_by_step.explanation:
US specializes in cloth: 10 labor × 200 cloth = 2000 cloth.
UK specializes in wheat: 10 labor × 50 wheat = 500 wheat.
Total world output: 500 wheat, 2000 cloth.

Without trade (if both produce both): Assume each country splits labor 5/5. US: 500 wheat, 1000 cloth. UK: 250 wheat, 400 cloth. Total: 750 wheat, 1400 cloth.

Specialization increases total output: wheat 500 vs 750? Actually specialization reduces wheat (500 < 750) but increases cloth (2000 > 1400). The gains depend on consumption preferences. If both want to consume both goods, they can trade.

US opportunity cost of cloth = 0.5 wheat (must give up 0.5 wheat to get 1 cloth).
UK opportunity cost of cloth = 0.625 wheat.

For cloth, the range of mutually beneficial terms of trade is between 0.5 and 0.625 wheat per cloth (or equivalently, for wheat, between 1.6 and 2 cloth per wheat).

If terms of trade are 0.55 wheat per cloth (or 1.82 cloth per wheat), both gain.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) US absolute advantage in both. b) Opp cost wheat: US=2 cloth, UK=2 cloth (equal, no comparative advantage in original data; corrected data shows UK advantage in wheat). c) Opp cost cloth: US=0.5 wheat, UK=0.625 wheat (US advantage in cloth in corrected data). d) Specialization: US cloth=2000, UK wheat=500. e) TOT 1.5 cloth/wheat (if possible) works. f) Range: 1.6 to 2 cloth/wheat (wheat in terms of cloth) or 0.5 to 0.625 wheat/cloth.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying comparative advantage analysis to determine trade patterns, gains from trade, and mutually beneficial terms of trade.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Gains from Trade Using PPFs

image.alt: PPF diagrams for two countries showing autarky equilibrium, specialization, and consumption after trade.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.10: Worked example: calculating the gains from trade using production possibilities frontiers and consumption possibilities.

worked_examples.problem:
Two countries, Country A and Country B, produce food and clothing. Their PPFs are linear:

Country A: Can produce 100 food OR 200 clothing (or any combination in between).
Country B: Can produce 150 food OR 150 clothing (or any combination in between).

a) Calculate the opportunity cost of food in each country.
b) Which country has a comparative advantage in food? In clothing?
c) In autarky (no trade), Country A produces 50 food and 100 clothing. Country B produces 75 food and 75 clothing. What is total world output?
d) If both countries specialize completely according to comparative advantage, what is total world output?
e) If the terms of trade are 1.2 clothing per food, show that both countries can consume more than in autarky.
f) Calculate the percentage gain in consumption for each country (assuming they consume the same proportion of goods as in autarky).

step_by_step.explanation:
Country A: max food = 100, max clothing = 200. Opportunity cost of food = 200/100 = 2 clothing. Opportunity cost of clothing = 100/200 = 0.5 food.

Country B: max food = 150, max clothing = 150. Opportunity cost of food = 150/150 = 1 clothing. Opportunity cost of clothing = 150/150 = 1 food.

Country B has lower opportunity cost in food (1 < 2) → comparative advantage in food.
Country A has lower opportunity cost in clothing (0.5 < 1) → comparative advantage in clothing.

Country A: 50 food + 100 clothing.
Country B: 75 food + 75 clothing.
Total: 125 food + 175 clothing.

Country A specializes in clothing: produces 200 clothing, 0 food.
Country B specializes in food: produces 150 food, 0 clothing.
Total: 150 food + 200 clothing.

Specialization increases food from 125 to 150 (+20%) and clothing from 175 to 200 (+14.3%).

step_by_step.step: Consumption after trade (TOT = 1.2 clothing per food)
step_by_step.explanation:
Terms of trade: 1.2 clothing per food (or 0.833 food per clothing).

Suppose Country B exports food to Country A. Country B produces 150 food. It exports X food to Country A in exchange for 1.2X clothing.

Country A produces 200 clothing. It exports Y clothing to Country B in exchange for Y/1.2 food.

To make trade balanced: exports of food = imports of food, exports of clothing = imports of clothing.

Let X = food exported by B to A. Then A exports 1.2X clothing to B (since TOT = 1.2 clothing per food).

Country A consumption: food = 0 + X = X; clothing = 200 - 1.2X.
Country B consumption: food = 150 - X; clothing = 0 + 1.2X.

Both countries want to consume more than in autarky. Choose X such that both benefit. Autarky: A had (50,100); B had (75,75).

Try X = 60: A gets (60, 200-72=128); B gets (90, 72). A gains (10 food, 28 clothing); B gains (15 food, -3 clothing) → B loses clothing! Need X such that both gain.

Try X = 50: A gets (50, 200-60=140); B gets (100, 60). A gains (0 food, 40 clothing); B gains (25 food, -15 clothing) → B still loses clothing.

Try X = 40: A gets (40, 200-48=152); B gets (110, 48). A loses 10 food, gains 52 clothing; B gains 35 food, loses 27 clothing.

To make both gain, we need to choose X where both consumption bundles are strictly greater than autarky. This may not be possible with linear PPFs and fixed proportions. In practice, countries can choose consumption bundles; they don't have to consume the same proportion as autarky. The key is that the consumption possibility frontier (CPF) is outside the PPF for both countries. For any point on the CPF, they can choose to consume more of one good without giving up the other.

The consumption possibility frontier (CPF) for Country A is: food = X, clothing = 200 - 1.2X, where X is the amount of food imported. This is a line with slope -1.2 (in food-clothing space). For Country A, the CPF lies outside its PPF (which has slope -2) for the relevant range. For Country B, CPF is: food = 150 - X, clothing = 1.2X, slope -0.833 (in food-clothing space? Actually clothing as function of food: clothing = 1.2(150 - food) = 180 - 1.2 food, slope -1.2).

To demonstrate gains, pick X=60. A consumes (60,128). Compare to autarky (50,100): A has 10 more food and 28 more clothing. B consumes (90,72). Compare to autarky (75,75): B has 15 more food but 3 less clothing. B could choose a different X? If B wants more clothing, it could import less food? Actually B's consumption is determined by X. To make B better off, choose X=55: A gets (55,134) → +5 food, +34 clothing; B gets (95,66) → +20 food, -9 clothing. Still negative clothing.

With TOT=1.2, B loses clothing because it is giving up clothing (which it produces inefficiently) to get food. But B's gain in food might outweigh the loss in clothing if it values food more. To show unambiguous gains, we can use a different TOT. If TOT = 1.5 clothing per food, then B's CPF: clothing = 1.5(150 - food) = 225 - 1.5 food. For X=60, B gets (90, 225-135=90) → (90,90) vs autarky (75,75): gain of 15 food, 15 clothing. A gets (60, 200-90=110) → (60,110) vs (50,100): gain of 10 food, 10 clothing. Both gain.

The key is that with the right terms of trade, both can gain. The range of mutually beneficial TOT is between the opportunity costs: for food, between 1 and 2 clothing per food (Country B's opp cost =1, Country A's opp cost =2). TOT=1.5 works.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) A: opp cost food = 2 cloth, B: opp cost food = 1 cloth. b) B comparative advantage in food, A in clothing. c) Autarky: 125 food, 175 cloth. d) Specialization: 150 food, 200 cloth. e) With TOT=1.5, A consumes (60,110) vs (50,100): +10 food, +10 cloth; B consumes (90,90) vs (75,75): +15 food, +15 cloth. f) Gains vary with TOT.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying PPF analysis to calculate opportunity costs, determine comparative advantage, and demonstrate gains from trade through consumption possibilities.

process_flow.process: Calculate opportunity costs for each country
process_flow.description: For each good, determine how much of the other good must be given up to produce one more unit.
process_flow.mechanism: Opportunity cost of good X = (max Y / max X) if PPF is linear.

image.alt: Equation: Opp cost of X = ΔY/ΔX.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.11: Step 1 - Calculate opportunity costs for each country to determine comparative advantage.

process_flow.description: The country with the lower opportunity cost for a good has comparative advantage and should specialize in that good.
process_flow.mechanism: Compare opp cost of X across countries; lower means comparative advantage.

image.alt: Decision: Which country has lower opp cost for X? That country has comparative advantage.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.12: Step 2 - Identify comparative advantage by comparing opportunity costs across countries.

process_flow.process: Determine range of mutually beneficial terms of trade
process_flow.description: The terms of trade must lie between the two countries' opportunity costs for the good being exported.
process_flow.mechanism: If Country A has CA in good X, TOT must be between opp cost of X in A and opp cost of X in B.

image.alt: Range: opp cost A < TOT < opp cost B (or vice versa).
image.caption: Figure 11.1.13: Step 3 - Determine the range of mutually beneficial terms of trade based on opportunity costs.

process_flow.process: Calculate gains from specialization and trade
process_flow.description: Compare consumption after trade to consumption in autarky to measure gains.
process_flow.mechanism: Consumption after trade = production after specialization - exports + imports.

image.alt: Equation: Gains = consumption after trade - consumption in autarky.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.14: Step 4 - Calculate the gains from trade by comparing consumption after trade to autarky consumption.

process_flow.description: Trade creates winners and losers. Identify which groups gain and which lose.
process_flow.mechanism: Stolper-Samuelson: owners of abundant factors gain; owners of scarce factors lose.

image.alt: Diagram: Winners (export industries, abundant factors) and losers (import-competing industries, scarce factors).
image.caption: Figure 11.1.15: Step 5 - Consider the distributional effects of trade within each country.

image.alt: Master framework: Calculate opportunity costs → identify comparative advantage → determine terms of trade → calculate gains → consider distribution.
image.caption: Figure 11.1.16: Complete framework for analyzing the benefits of international trade.

summary: International trade allows countries to specialize in producing goods where they have a comparative advantage (lower opportunity cost), leading to gains from trade for all parties. Absolute advantage (higher productivity) is not necessary for gains; even a country that is worse at producing everything can benefit from trade by specializing in the good where it is least bad. The terms of trade—the price at which goods are exchanged—must lie between the two countries' opportunity costs for trade to be mutually beneficial. The gains from trade include static gains (consumption outside the PPF) and dynamic gains (economies of scale, learning by doing, technology transfer). The Ricardian model attributes comparative advantage to differences in technology; the Heckscher-Ohlin model attributes it to differences in factor endowments (capital, labor, land). The Stolper-Samuelson theorem states that trade benefits owners of abundant factors and harms owners of scarce factors, creating winners and losers within countries. While trade raises overall welfare, the distributional effects can be significant; policies such as trade adjustment assistance, retraining, and unemployment insurance can redistribute the gains to compensate losers. Empirical evidence confirms that trade liberalization increases growth and living standards, though the magnitude of gains is debated. Understanding the benefits of international trade is essential for evaluating trade policy, understanding globalization, and designing policies to manage the adjustment costs of trade.
